Node IDs nodelist outputs match /columns corresponding edgelist, can passed directly igraph::graph_from_data_frame().

title: CLAUDE.md
text: vector: weights = TRUE collapses duplicate (, ) pairs count. nodelist() always returns unique values frequency n column. data.frame: weights = TRUE collapses fully identical rows (columns must match, just /) adds weight column. separate symmetric_cols + dedupe, normalizes edge direction. list: Edges structurally unique (one edge per parent-child pair); duplicates occur. randomForest / tree / rpart / xgboost / gbm: Edges structurally unique (tree topology); duplicates occur.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: S3 method lives file: R/edgelist.R (generic), R/edgelist.data.frame.R, R/edgelist.randomForest.R, etc. pattern nodelist.*. Graph conversion methods live R/.igraph.R (methods igraph::.igraph) R/as_tbl_graph.R (methods tidygraph::as_tbl_graph). Internal helpers shared across nodelist methods live R/utils-nodelist.R (.compute_depth(), .compute_dev_improvement()).

title: CLAUDE.md
text: vector (default): Sequential edges: element connects element + 1, producing n - 1 edges length-n vector. list: Recursive traversal nested list structure. element produces parent-child edge. Path-style IDs (root//b) ensure uniqueness. Named elements use names; unnamed elements use positional indices ([[1]]). max_depth limits recursion. S3 objects without dedicated method fall edgelist.default via .list() check unclassed traversal. randomForest: Iterates trees via randomForest::getTree(), identifies parent nodes (left_daughter != 0), creates edges children. treenum filters specific trees. tree: Binary heap node IDs rownames(frame) (root=1, left=2k, right=2k+1). Parent-child edges derived via id %/% 2. Split labels parsed components (split_var, split_op, split_point) splits matrix. data.frame: Cartesian product source/target column pairs. Builds edge blocks na.rm filtering, optional directed column symmetric_cols, direction-based dedup, row-level dedup via weights. rpart: binary heap approach tree (root=1, left=2k, right=2k+1). Edge labels labels(input_object, collapse = TRUE) handles ncat sign correctly. Uses < >= operators (unlike tree’s </>). xgboost: Uses xgb.model.dt.tree() data.table. Split nodes explicit Yes/ID columns (string format "Tree-Node"). phantom nodes. gbm: Uses pretty.gbm.tree(). Must exclude missing-sentinel nodes (phantom routing nodes NA handling). Node IDs 0-based integers. Multinomial models store n.trees * num.classes physical trees.

title: Complete method reference
text: Vectors produce sequential edges: element connects element + 1.

title: Complete method reference
text: Numeric vectors work way: Duplicate edges can collapsed weights = TRUE:
code: edgelist(c("A", "B", "C", "D")) #>   from to #> 1    A  B #> 2    B  C #> 3    C  D edgelist(c(1, 2, 3)) #>   from to #> 1    1  2 #> 2    2  3 edgelist(c("A", "B", "A", "B", "C"), weights = TRUE) #>   from to weight #> 1    A  B      2 #> 2    B  A      1 #> 3    B  C      1

title: Complete method reference
text: Returns unique values frequency counts:
code: nodelist(c("A", "B", "A", "B", "C")) #>   name n #> 1    A 2 #> 2    B 2 #> 3    C 1

title: Complete method reference
text: Lists produce recursive parent-child edges. element becomes node path-style ID.

title: Complete method reference
text: Unnamed elements use positional indices: Limit depth (root = 0, children = 1, …):
code: edgelist(list(a = list(b = 1, c = 2), d = 3)) #>     from       to depth #> 1   root   root/a     1 #> 2 root/a root/a/b     2 #> 3 root/a root/a/c     2 #> 4   root   root/d     1 edgelist(list(1, 2, list(3, 4))) #>         from               to depth #> 1       root       root/[[1]]     1 #> 2       root       root/[[2]]     1 #> 3       root       root/[[3]]     1 #> 4 root/[[3]] root/[[3]]/[[1]]     2 #> 5 root/[[3]] root/[[3]]/[[2]]     2 edgelist(list(a = list(b = list(c = 1))), max_depth = 2) #>     from       to depth #> 1   root   root/a     1 #> 2 root/a root/a/b     2

title: Complete method reference
code: nodelist(list(a = list(b = 1, c = 2), d = 3)) #>       name depth    type n_children label #> 1     root     0    list          2  root #> 2   root/a     1    list          2     a #> 3 root/a/b     2 numeric          0     b #> 4 root/a/c     2 numeric          0     c #> 5   root/d     1 numeric          0     d

code: library(igraph)  all_edges <- edgelist(courses, source_cols = course,                       target_cols = c(prereq, prereq2, crosslist),                       attr_cols = c(),                       symmetric_cols = crosslist)  # Reverse prereq arrows so they point from prereq -> dependent course directed_rows <- all_edges$directed all_edges[directed_rows, c("from", "to")] <- all_edges[directed_rows, c("to", "from")]  nodes <- nodelist(courses, id_col = course) g <- graph_from_data_frame(all_edges, vertices = nodes)

title: networkformat
text: Many R objects contain network structure isn’t standard format. Data frames encode relationships columns — prerequisites, co-occurrences, categorical groupings — can form graphs. Tree-based models store splits internal matrices node indices. Nested lists imply parent-child hierarchies. Getting form igraph, tidygraph, ggraph can work typically means writing bespoke reshaping code. networkformat reshaping . extracts standard edgelists (-pairs) nodelists (node attributes) data frames, tree-based models, lists, vectors, putting everything format rest R network ecosystem expects.

title: networkformat
text: simplest case — vector becomes chain edges: Five lines tree model plot:
code: library(networkformat)  edgelist(c("start", "middle", "end")) #>     from     to #> 1  start middle #> 2 middle    end nodelist(c("A", "B", "A", "C")) #>   name n #> 1    A 2 #> 2    B 1 #> 3    C 1 library(networkformat) library(tidygraph) library(ggraph)  tr <- tree::tree(Species ~ Sepal.Length + Sepal.Width, data = iris) tg <- as_tbl_graph(tr)  ggraph(tg, layout = "tree") +   geom_edge_diagonal(colour = "grey60") +   geom_node_label(aes(label = label, fill = ifelse(is_leaf, yval, var)),                   size = 2.5, colour = "white", fontface = "bold") +   scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2", name = NULL) +   theme_graph(base_family = "sans") +   labs(title = "Iris classification tree")

title: networkformat
text: data edgelist/nodelist form, large ecosystem packages available, including: igraph — workhorse graph library R. Handles graph construction, shortest paths, centrality, community detection, clustering, much . Also converts adjacency matrices (as_adjacency_matrix()) incidence matrices (as_incidence_matrix()) linear algebra approaches. networkformat provides .igraph() methods tree-based models (tree, rpart, randomForest, xgb.Booster, gbm), can go model igraph one step. tidygraph — tidy interface built top igraph. Lets manipulate graphs dplyr verbs (mutate, filter, arrange) keeping underlying igraph structure. ’re comfortable tidyverse, natural way work graphs. networkformat provides as_tbl_graph() methods tree-based models, tidygraph’s as_tbl_graph.data.frame() auto-detects edge data frames /columns — edgelist networkformat, can pass directly tidygraph well. ggraph — ggplot2 extension graph visualization, designed work tidygraph/igraph objects. Supports tree layouts, force-directed layouts, circular layouts, . network / statnet — alternative graph ecosystem focused statistical network modeling (ERGMs, latent space models). igraph objects can converted network objects via intergraph::asNetwork(). networkformat gets data ecosystem. , can visualize, compute statistics, fit models, convert matrices — whatever analysis requires.

title: networkformat
text: visualizing tree models quickly, see vignette("visualization"). working directly edgelists nodelists — including available arguments data frame workflow — see vignette("edgelist-nodelist").

title: Visual QA: every model type plotted
code: tr <- tree::tree(Species ~ Sepal.Length + Sepal.Width, data = iris) tg <- as_tbl_graph(tr) %>%   mutate(fill_var = ifelse(is_leaf, yval, var))  node_df <- as.data.frame(tg, what = "vertices") vars    <- setdiff(unique(node_df$var), "<leaf>") classes <- unique(node_df$yval[node_df$is_leaf]) pal <- c(   setNames(c("#3B8EA5", "#D4A373"), vars[seq_len(min(length(vars), 2))]),   setNames(c("#66c2a5", "#fc8d62", "#8da0cb"), classes[seq_len(min(length(classes), 3))]) )  ggraph(tg, layout = "tree") +   geom_edge_diagonal(colour = "grey60", width = 0.5) +   geom_edge_diagonal(     aes(label = ifelse(is.na(split_point), "", paste0(split_op, split_point))),     angle_calc = "along", label_dodge = unit(3, "mm"),     label_size = 2.5, label_colour = "grey30", colour = NA) +   geom_node_label(     aes(label = label, fill = fill_var),     size = 2.5, colour = "white", fontface = "bold",     label.padding = unit(0.25, "lines"), label.r = unit(0.2, "lines")) +   scale_fill_manual(values = pal, name = NULL) +   theme_graph(base_family = "sans") +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   labs(title = "Decision tree (tree)",        subtitle = "Nodes coloured by split variable / predicted class")

title: Visual QA: every model type plotted
code: rp <- rpart::rpart(Species ~ ., data = iris) tg_rp <- as_tbl_graph(rp) %>%   mutate(fill_var = ifelse(is_leaf, yval, var))  node_df <- as.data.frame(tg_rp, what = "vertices") vars    <- setdiff(unique(node_df$var), "<leaf>") classes <- unique(node_df$yval[node_df$is_leaf]) pal_rp <- c(   setNames(c("#3B8EA5", "#D4A373", "#7B68AE", "#E07B54")[seq_along(vars)], vars),   setNames(c("#66c2a5", "#fc8d62", "#8da0cb")[seq_along(classes)], classes) )  ggraph(tg_rp, layout = "tree") +   geom_edge_diagonal(colour = "grey60", width = 0.5) +   geom_edge_diagonal(     aes(label = ifelse(is.na(split_point), "", paste0(split_op, split_point))),     angle_calc = "along", label_dodge = unit(3, "mm"),     label_size = 2.5, label_colour = "grey30", colour = NA) +   geom_node_label(     aes(label = label, fill = fill_var),     size = 2.5, colour = "white", fontface = "bold",     label.padding = unit(0.25, "lines"), label.r = unit(0.2, "lines")) +   scale_fill_manual(values = pal_rp, name = NULL) +   theme_graph(base_family = "sans") +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   labs(title = "Recursive partitioning (rpart)",        subtitle = "Note rpart uses >= operator convention (vs tree's >)")

title: Visual QA: every model type plotted

code: data(courses) all_edges <- edgelist(courses,                       source_cols = c(prereq, prereq2, crosslist),                       target_cols = course) all_edges$directed <- all_edges$from_col != "crosslist" all_edges <- all_edges[all_edges$directed | all_edges$from < all_edges$to, ]  nodes <- nodelist(courses, id_col = course) g <- graph_from_data_frame(all_edges, vertices = nodes) tg_courses <- as_tbl_graph(g)  dept_pal <- c(STAT = "#5B8DB8", MATH = "#E07B54", DATA = "#6BAF6B", CS = "#9B6BB0")  ggraph(tg_courses, layout = "stress") +   geom_edge_link(     aes(filter = directed),     arrow = arrow(length = unit(2, "mm"), type = "closed"),     end_cap = circle(8, "mm"), width = 0.5, colour = "grey45") +   geom_edge_link(     aes(filter = !directed),     end_cap = circle(6, "mm"), start_cap = circle(6, "mm"),     width = 0.8, colour = "#9B59B6", linetype = "dashed") +   geom_node_label(     aes(label = name, fill = dept),     colour = "white", fontface = "bold", size = 3,     label.padding = unit(0.3, "lines"), label.r = unit(0.2, "lines")) +   scale_fill_manual(values = dept_pal, name = "Department") +   theme_graph(base_family = "sans") +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   labs(title = "Course prerequisite network",        subtitle = "Arrows = prerequisites | Dashed = crosslisted")

title: Visualizing models as networks
text: graph carries attributes need plotting — node labels, split variables, leaf predictions, observation counts, parsed edge thresholds.  Internal nodes colored split variable. Leaf nodes show predicted class. Edge labels show split condition (e.g. <5.45).
code: tr <- tree::tree(Species ~ Sepal.Length + Sepal.Width, data = iris) tg <- as_tbl_graph(tr) cat("Nodes:", igraph::vcount(tg), " Edges:", igraph::ecount(tg),     " Depth:", max(igraph::distances(tg, mode = "out")[1, ]), "\n") #> Nodes: 15  Edges: 14  Depth: 4 # Add a fill column: split variable for internal nodes, predicted class for leaves tg <- tg %>%   mutate(fill_var = ifelse(is_leaf, yval, var))  # Build a palette: one color per variable + one per class node_df <- as.data.frame(tg, what = "vertices") vars <- setdiff(unique(node_df$var), "<leaf>") classes <- unique(node_df$yval[node_df$is_leaf]) var_pal <- c(   setNames(c("#3B8EA5", "#D4A373"), vars[seq_len(min(length(vars), 2))]),   setNames(c("#66c2a5", "#fc8d62", "#8da0cb"), classes[seq_len(min(length(classes), 3))]) )  ggraph(tg, layout = "tree") +   geom_edge_diagonal(colour = "grey60", width = 0.5) +   geom_edge_diagonal(     aes(label = ifelse(is.na(split_point), "", paste0(split_op, split_point))),     angle_calc = "along", label_dodge = unit(3, "mm"),     label_size = 2.5, label_colour = "grey30", colour = NA) +   geom_node_label(     aes(label = label, fill = fill_var),     size = 2.5, colour = "white", fontface = "bold",     label.padding = unit(0.25, "lines"), label.r = unit(0.2, "lines")) +   scale_fill_manual(values = var_pal, name = NULL) +   theme_graph(base_family = "sans") +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   labs(title = "Iris classification tree",        subtitle = "Split on Sepal.Length and Sepal.Width")

title: Visualizing models as networks
text: as_tbl_graph(rf) combines trees single graph disconnected components — one per tree. node carries treenum attribute can distinguish .  three trees visible single plot. Internal nodes colored split variable; leaf nodes colored predicted class.
code: rf <- randomForest::randomForest(Species ~ ., data = iris, ntree = 3, maxnodes = 5)  # All 3 trees in one graph tg_rf <- as_tbl_graph(rf) cat("Nodes:", igraph::vcount(tg_rf), " Edges:", igraph::ecount(tg_rf),     " Components:", igraph::count_components(tg_rf), "\n") #> Nodes: 27  Edges: 24  Components: 3 # Map integer prediction indices to class names tg_rf <- tg_rf %>%   mutate(     pred_class = rf$classes[prediction],     display_label = ifelse(       is_leaf, pred_class,       paste0(split_var_name, "\n< ", round(split_point, 1))     ),     fill_var = ifelse(is_leaf, pred_class, split_var_name)   )  # Palette: one color per predictor variable + one per predicted class node_df <- as.data.frame(tg_rf, what = "vertices") predictor_vars <- sort(unique(na.omit(node_df$split_var_name))) leaf_classes <- sort(unique(na.omit(node_df$pred_class))) var_colors <- c("#3B8EA5", "#D4A373", "#7B68AE", "#E07B54") class_colors <- c("#66c2a5", "#fc8d62", "#8da0cb") var_pal_rf <- c(   setNames(var_colors[seq_along(predictor_vars)], predictor_vars),   setNames(class_colors[seq_along(leaf_classes)], leaf_classes) )  ggraph(tg_rf, layout = "tree") +   geom_edge_link(     arrow = arrow(length = unit(1.5, "mm"), type = "closed"),     end_cap = circle(5, "mm"),     colour = "grey70", width = 0.3) +   geom_node_label(     aes(label = display_label, fill = fill_var),     size = 2.5, colour = "black", fontface = "bold",     label.padding = unit(0.2, "lines"), label.r = unit(0.15, "lines"),     alpha = 0.85) +   scale_fill_manual(values = var_pal_rf, name = NULL) +   theme_graph(base_family = "sans") +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   labs(title = "Random forest (3 trees)",        subtitle = "Each disconnected component is one tree")

title: Visualizing models as networks

code: tg1 <- as_tbl_graph(rf, treenum = 1) %>%   mutate(     pred_class = rf$classes[prediction],     display_label = ifelse(       is_leaf, pred_class,       paste0(split_var_name, "\n< ", round(split_point, 1))     ),     fill_var = ifelse(is_leaf, pred_class, split_var_name)   )  ggraph(tg1, layout = "dendrogram") +   geom_edge_elbow(colour = "grey65", width = 0.4) +   geom_node_label(     aes(label = display_label, fill = fill_var),     colour = "black", size = 2.5, fontface = "bold",     label.padding = unit(0.25, "lines"), label.r = unit(0.2, "lines"),     label.size = 0.4, alpha = 0.2) +   geom_node_text(     aes(label = display_label),     colour = "black", size = 2.5, fontface = "bold") +   scale_fill_manual(values = var_pal_rf, name = NULL) +   theme_graph(base_family = "sans") +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   labs(title = "Tree 1 from the random forest") #> Warning in geom_node_label(aes(label = display_label, fill = fill_var), : #> Ignoring unknown parameters: `label.size`

title: Visualizing models as networks
text: networkformat also handles tabular data. bundled courses dataset contains 13 courses across 4 departments, prerequisite crosslist relationships.  full data frame workflow — column selection, na.rm, attr_cols, symmetric_cols, graph statistics — see vignette("edgelist-nodelist").
code: library(igraph)  # Prereqs as source so arrows naturally point prereq -> course; # crosslist is undirected, so its direction doesn't matter all_edges <- edgelist(courses,                       source_cols = c(prereq, prereq2, crosslist),                       target_cols = course) all_edges$directed <- all_edges$from_col != "crosslist" all_edges <- all_edges[all_edges$directed | all_edges$from < all_edges$to, ]  nodes <- nodelist(courses, id_col = course) g <- graph_from_data_frame(all_edges, vertices = nodes) tg_courses <- as_tbl_graph(g)  cat("Nodes:", vcount(g), " Edges:", ecount(g), "\n") #> Nodes: 13  Edges: 19  dept_pal <- c(STAT = "#5B8DB8", MATH = "#E07B54", DATA = "#6BAF6B", CS = "#9B6BB0")  ggraph(tg_courses, layout = "stress") +   geom_edge_link(     aes(filter = directed),     arrow = arrow(length = unit(2, "mm"), type = "closed"),     end_cap = circle(8, "mm"),     width = 0.5, colour = "grey45") +   geom_edge_link(     aes(filter = !directed),     end_cap = circle(6, "mm"), start_cap = circle(6, "mm"),     width = 0.8, colour = "#9B59B6", linetype = "dashed") +   geom_node_label(     aes(label = name, fill = dept),     colour = "white", fontface = "bold", size = 3,     label.padding = unit(0.3, "lines"), label.r = unit(0.2, "lines")) +   scale_fill_manual(values = dept_pal, name = "Department") +   theme_graph(base_family = "sans") +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   labs(title = "Course prerequisite network",        subtitle = "Arrows point from prerequisite to dependent course  |  Dashed = crosslisted")

title: Convert R Objects to Network Edgelists and Nodelists
text: networkformat converts R objects network edgelist/nodelist format visualization analysis packages like igraph, tidygraph, ggraph. works tree-based ML models (randomForest, tree, rpart, xgboost, gbm), data frames, vectors. Full documentation https://jesseabrandt.github.io/networkformat/.

title: Convert R Objects to Network Edgelists and Nodelists
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("jesseabrandt/networkformat")

title: Convert R Objects to Network Edgelists and Nodelists
code: library(networkformat)  # Vectors --- sequential chain of edges edgelist(c("A", "B", "C", "D")) #>   from to #> 1    A  B #> 2    B  C #> 3    C  D  nodelist(c("A", "B", "A", "C")) #>   name n #> 1    A 2 #> 2    B 1 #> 3    C 1  # Data frames --- column pairs become edges edgelist(courses, source_cols = course, target_cols = prereq)  # Tree models --- one-step graph conversion library(tree) tr <- tree(Species ~ ., data = iris) as.igraph(tr)

title: Convert R Objects to Network Edgelists and Nodelists
text: atomic vector becomes sequential edgelist: element connects element + 1.
code: edgelist(c("intro", "basics", "advanced", "project")) #>       from       to #> 1    intro   basics #> 2   basics advanced #> 3 advanced  project  # Collapse duplicate edges with a count edgelist(c("A", "B", "A", "B", "C"), weights = TRUE) #>   from to weight #> 1    A  B      2 #> 2    B  A      1 #> 3    B  C      1  # Node list gives unique values and frequencies nodelist(c("A", "B", "A", "B", "C")) #>   name n #> 1    A 2 #> 2    B 2 #> 3    C 1

title: Convert R Objects to Network Edgelists and Nodelists
text: Specify columns source/target nodes. columns carried edge attributes default. Key parameters: na.rm = TRUE (default) — remove edges NA symmetric_cols — mark target columns undirected; adds directed column dedupe = TRUE (default) — using symmetric_cols, keep one direction (<= ) weights = TRUE — collapse identical rows add weight count column
code: # Basic edgelist from two columns edgelist(courses, source_cols = course, target_cols = prereq)  # Multiple target columns (Cartesian product of source x target) edgelist(courses, source_cols = course, target_cols = c(prereq, crosslist))  # Keep only specific attribute columns edgelist(courses, source_cols = course, target_cols = prereq,          attr_cols = c(dept, credits))  # Symmetric (undirected) edges with deduplication edgelist(courses, source_cols = course,          target_cols = c(prereq, crosslist),          symmetric_cols = crosslist)  # Collapse duplicate rows and count them edgelist(courses, source_cols = course, target_cols = prereq,          weights = TRUE)

what: networkformat: Convert R Objects to Network Edgelists and Nodelists — networkformat-package
title: networkformat: Convert R Objects to Network Edgelists and Nodelists — networkformat-package
text: Converts R objects network edgelist nodelist format visualization analysis packages like 'igraph', 'tidygraph', 'ggraph'. Works data frames (column-pair edges tidyselect), tree-based model objects ('randomForest', 'tree', 'rpart', 'xgboost', 'gbm'), lists (recursive parent-child edges), atomic vectors (sequential edges). Also provides one-step graph construction via .igraph() as_tbl_graph() methods tree models.
